div1.entryFree: Brake, thicket: round rising hillocks, --s obscure and rough, Ven. 237. hasting to feed her fawn hid in some b. Ven. 237 kennelled in a b. Ven. 237 Pilgr. 126. Mids. II, 1, 227. III, 1, 4. III, 1, 4 III, 1, 4 III, 2, 15. H6C III, 1, 1. the rough b. that virtue must go through, H8 I, 2, 75. -- Some run from brakes of Ice, and answer none, and some condemned for a fault alone, Meas. II, 1, 39, a passage, as it seems, hopelessly corrupt. Some M. Edd. write breaks of ice, others brakes of vice. So much is certain, that the idea hidden in the words brakes of Ice must be antithetical to a fault alone.
